coredata.dc:title: Sensitive fluorometric determination of picric acid and antioxidant stabilizers in propellant compositions using amine-rich nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

Üzer, Ayşem
Apak, Reşat
coredata.dc:description: Determination of both trace explosives in soil samples and antioxidant stabilizers in propellant compositions using the same quantum dots is an important challenge in analytical chemistry. For this purpose, unmodified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared with surface heteroatom (nitrogen and oxygen) doped, water-soluble and highly fluorescent properties. The CQDs were synthesized by reflux method using citric acid (CA) and N-(4-methylpyridin-2-yl)ethane-1,2-diamine (NMPE) as carbon- and nitrogen-sources, respectively, and displayed a quantum yield as high as 48.30%. Surface morphology of CQDs was determined by STEM, IR, Raman and XPS techniques, and optical properties were determined by fluorescence spectroscopy, fluorescence lifetime measurement, and UV–vis spectroscopy. CQDs, excited at 310 nm, have a spherical structure with an average diameter of 2.0 nm, and emit strong blue fluorescence at a wavelength of 380 nm. With the static fluorescence quenching phenomenon based on the internal filter effect (IFE) mechanism, CQDs can precisely detect picric acid (PA), curcumin, and amine-type antioxidants used as stabilizers (i.e. N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine, N-phenyl-1-naphthylamine, N-phenyl-2-naphthylamine, and diphenylamine). With detection limits of 0.5 nmol L −1 for picric acid, 70 nmol L −1 for curcumin, and 600 nmol L −1 for N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine, CQDs can detect analytes with good recovery in the range of 95% to 105% in the presence of different explosives (i.e., tetryl, TNT, PETN, HMX, and RDX for picric acid), metal ions (Cd2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, and Ca2+ for picric acid and curcumin), anions (Cl−, NO3 −, NO2 −, SO4 2−, and CO3 2− for picric acid and curcumin), biomolecules (glucose, trypsin, lysine, l-phenylalanine, and DL-alanine for curcumin), and camouflage materials (i.e. paracetamol, glucose, detergent, aspartame, and acetyl salicylic acid for picric acid). The developed method was validated against LC-MS/MS (for picric acid) and spectrophotometric CUPRAC (for curcumin) by comparatively analyzing picric acid in contaminated sandy soil and curcumin in synthetically prepared propellant composition.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Florescence excitation, emission, and UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs, and UV–vis absorption spectra of picric acid, curcumin, and N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine (the insets showed CQDs solution under daylight and UV-light at 365 nm). b) Photostability measurements of the CQDs solution (0.05 g mL−1).

a) STEM image of CQDs (the scale is 40 nm) (The inset shows the STEM image of CQDs with 10 nm zoom). b) Size distribution histogram of CQDs by DLS. c) Infrared (KBr pellet) and d) Raman spectra of CQDs.
a) XPS spectrum of CQDs. High resolution spectrum of b) C1s, c) O1s, and d) N1s.

a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of PA (from 1.0 × 10−8 mol L −1 to 1.4 × 10−7 mol L −1). b) The relationship between I 0/I and increasing concentrations of PA, where I 0 and I are the fluorescence intensities of CQDs in the absence and presence of picric acid, respectively. c) The double-reciprocal Benesi-Hildebrand plot of the CQDs in the presence of picric acid.
Fluorescence a) spectra and b) intensity histogram of CQDs in the presence of various explosives.

a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of curcumin (from 0.5 × 10−6 mol L −1to 1.0 × 10−5 mol L −1). b) The relationship between I 0-I and increasing concentrations of curcumin, where I 0 and I are the fluorescence intensities of CQDs in the absence and presence of curcumin, respectively. c) Benesi-Hildebrand double-reciprocal plot of the CQDs in presence of curcumin.

a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine (from 0.5 × 10−6 mol L −1 to 5.0 × 10−6 mol L −1). b) The relationship between I 0-I and increasing concentration of N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine, where I 0 and I are the fluorescence intensities of CQDs in the absence and presence of N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine, respectively. c) Benesi-Hildebrand double-reciprocal plot of the CQDs in presence of N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine.

Fluorescence lifetime decay and Stern-Volmer (inset) curves of a) CQDs and CQDs+picric acid, b) CQDs and CQDS+curcumin, and c) CQDs and CQDs+N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine (PA: picric aicd, CUR: curcumin, PPDa: N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine).

a) Fluorescence intensity of CQDs without picric acid (orange bars) and with picric acid (green bars) in the presence of different metal ions (100-fold), anions (100-fold), and camouflage materials (10-fold) (B: blank, 1-Cd2+, 2-Fe2+, 3-Mg2+, 4-Cu2+, 5-Zn2+, 6-Mn2+, 7-Ca2+, 8-Cl−, 9-NO3 −, 10-NO2 −, 11-SO4 2−, and 12-CO3 2−, 13-paracetamol, 14-glucose, 15-detergent, 16-aspartame, and 17-acetyl salicylic acid). b) Fluorescence intensity of CQDs without curcumin (blue bars) and with curcumin (pink bars) in the presence of different metal ions (10-fold), anions (10-fold), and biomolecules (10-fold) (B: blank, 1-Cd2+, 2-Fe2+, 3-Mg2+, 4-Cu2+, 5-Zn2+, 6-Mn2+, 7-Ca2+, 8-Cl−, 9-NO3 −, 10-NO2 −, 11-SO4 2−, and 12-CO3 2−, 13-glucose, 14-trypsin, 15-lysine, 16-l-phenylalanine, and 17-DL-alanine).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Determination of both trace explosives in soil samples and antioxidant stabilizers in propellant compositions using the same quantum dots is an important challenge in analytical chemistry. For this purpose, unmodified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared with surface heteroatom (nitrogen and oxygen) doped, water-soluble and highly fluorescent properties. The CQDs were synthesized by reflux method using citric acid (CA) and N-(4-methylpyridin-2-yl)ethane-1,2-diamine (NMPE) as carbon- and nitrogen-sources, respectively, and displayed a quantum yield as high as 48.30%. Surface morphology of CQDs was determined by STEM, IR, Raman and XPS techniques, and optical properties were determined by fluorescence spectroscopy, fluorescence lifetime measurement, and UV–vis spectroscopy. CQDs, excited at 310 nm, have a spherical structure with an average diameter of 2.0 nm, and emit strong blue fluorescence at a wavelength of 380 nm. With the static fluorescence quenching phenomenon based on the internal filter effect (IFE) mechanism, CQDs can precisely detect picric acid (PA), curcumin, and amine-type antioxidants used as stabilizers (i.e. N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine, N-phenyl-1-naphthylamine, N-phenyl-2-naphthylamine, and diphenylamine). With detection limits of 0.5 nmol L −1 for picric acid, 70 nmol L −1 for curcumin, and 600 nmol L −1 for N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine, CQDs can detect analytes with good recovery in the range of 95% to 105% in the presence of different explosives (i.e., tetryl, TNT, PETN, HMX, and RDX for picric acid), metal ions (Cd2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, and Ca2+ for picric acid and curcumin), anions (Cl−, NO3 −, NO2 −, SO4 2−, and CO3 2− for picric acid and curcumin), biomolecules (glucose, trypsin, lysine, l-phenylalanine, and DL-alanine for curcumin), and camouflage materials (i.e. paracetamol, glucose, detergent, aspartame, and acetyl salicylic acid for picric acid). The developed method was validated against LC-MS/MS (for picric acid) and spectrophotometric CUPRAC (for curcumin) by comparatively analyzing picric acid in contaminated sandy soil and curcumin in synthetically prepared propellant composition.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, well-organized terrorist attacks around the world have prompted researchers to find more effective ways to detect explosives [1,2]. Many common explosives contain nitro-compounds as primary ingredients [3]. Trinitrotoluene (TNT) and trinitrophenol were the most popular among poly-nitroaromatic explosives until World War-I [4,5]. Trinitrophenol, also known as picric acid (PA), is a strong organic acid with a low pKa [6,7]. Like many poly-nitroaromatic compounds, it is a strong explosive [8], having explosive power even greater than that of TNT [9]. It was widely used in World War-I because of this powerful explosive feature [10]. Besides, picric acid is used in paint industry, pharmaceutical and chemical industry, fireworks industry, and matchbox production facilities [11,12]. Due to its high aqueous solubility enabling groundwater transport, picric acid can easily pollute the soil and water environment [13]. Picric acid also has toxicity for living organisms, manifested in various damages such as abnormal liver function [14], kidney failure [15], respiratory problems [16], skin-eye irritation [17]. In addition to these damages, picric acid is metabolically converted into picramic acid (2-amino-4,6-dinitrophenol), having a higher mutagenic activity than the parent compound [18]. It is primarily important to develop sensors (especially nanosensors having unique surface properties) that can detect picric acid even at very low concentrations due to its involvement in terrorist acts, potential environmental pollution, and the damage given to living organisms. Picric acid, having both electron-attracting –NO2 and electron-donating –OH groups, can interact with various probe molecules through charge-transfer and hydrogen-bonding complexes. Since picric acid is an acid of moderate-strength, it is monoanionic (picrate) at relatively low pH, enabling to form electrostatic complexes with large cations. Polyelectrolyte probes and picric acid have excellent solubility in aqueous media which brings the probe and analyte (picric acid) in close proximity via electrostatic attraction and facilitates efficient charge transfer and/or energy transfer mechanisms, thereby providing good fluorescence quenching towards picric acid [16], whereas other explosive compounds basically lack such properties. Considering the rich detection possibilities of picric acid, this work takes benefit of the formation of a (CQDs+picric acid) ground–state complex responsible for static quenching of the fluorescence intensity, giving rise to a sensitive and selective assay for picric acid.

Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [19], HPLC [20], and SPME-ion mobility spectrometry (IMS) [21] were used for the detection of picric acid. It may be discussed that these methods limit the practical application, are costly, involve long application procedures, and create difficulties in field applications. Compared to these conventional methods, nanoparticle− [22], polymer− [23], and MOF [24] −based chemical sensors have been developed in recent years, which stand out with their fast response, availability, portability and easy applicability [25]. Particularly carbon dots, constituting the most widely used group of nano-chemistry, show an increasing trend of popularity day by day [26]. It attracts the attention of researchers with its ease of synthesis, low cost, high photostability and quantum yield, high surface area and fluorescence properties [27].

Propellants are materials used to accelerate projectiles from weapons of all sizes and to launch rockets and missiles [28]. All nitrocellulose-based (NC-based) propellants are inherently unstable materials and decompose during aging through the evolution of nitroxyl radicals and nitrogen oxides [29]. NC-based propellants mainly degrade through unavoidable thermolysis and hydrolysis reactions, but their autocatalytic aging can be retarded by stabilizers [30] , extending the shelf-life of propellant. Commonly used stabilizer structures have aromatic rings that are more prone to nitration than other components in the propellant formulation [31]. Stabilizers traditionally used in propellants such as diphenylamine, 2-nitro-diphenylamine, ethylcentralite, and acardite-II are capable of converting the nitrous species formed during decomposition [32]. However, as carcinogenic species such as N-nitrosamines and N-nitrosamides may emerge during this transformation, the search for green stabilizers is on the rise [33]. For the analysis of monomeric amine stabilizers for polymeric material (including polymeric explosives) without major pretreatment steps, highly sophisticated spectroscopic techniques are employed, such as mass spectrometry (MS) coupled with desorption electrospray ionization (DESI)-MS, direct analysis in real time (DART)-MS, and matrix assisted laser desorption ionization (MALDI)-MS, which are costly, laborious, and require qualified personnel [34]. Aging and depletion kinetic modeling of contemporary stabilizers used in smokeless powder munition such as diphenylamine, 2-nitro-diphenylamine, akardite-II, and ethyl centralite are usually carried out for predicting the shelf life and storage conditions [35], but the starting compounds as well as degradation products cannot be analyzed with low-cost and practical methods. Although DeAtley described a spectrophotometric method for diphenylamine and its nitro-derivatives by oxidizing them with Fe(III) to a blue quinoneimide [36], the method requires the dangerous chemical sulfuric acid in concentrated solution and Fe(III) is capable to oxidize other phenolic preservatives and antioxidants to colored compounds that may interfere with the analysis. A recent review states that there is strong overlapping in the UV–Vis spectral determination of amine stabilizers, and evaluate that it is extremely difficult to analyze such compounds in mixtures without separation and purification [37]. Thus, there is an urgent need for simple and cheap spectroscopic methods for the analysis of amine stabilizers, forming the basis of the current work which makes use of CQDs in fluorometric analysis. On the other hand, curcumin (C21H20O6) is one of the cheapest and most abundant green stabilizers known [38] as it can absorb nitroxyl radicals. Curcumin obtained from turmeric roots is a natural antioxidant, and besides its antioxidant properties, it has antimutagenic, antitumor and anticarcinogenic properties [39].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which belong to a new class of carbon nanomaterials with dimensions below 10 nm, are becoming a rising star of researchers [40]. The main reason behind this popularity is their outstanding features, such as ease of preparation, surface functionalization, small particle size, biocompatibility, low toxicity, high quantum yield and high photostability [41]. CQDs have very good water solubility due to heteroatom doping on their surface [42]. Some of the applications of CQDs reported in the literature are bioimaging [43], medical applications [44], solar cells [45], catalyst [46], and chemical sensors [47].

Herein, we developed a CQDs−based method for the fluorometric determination of picric acid explosive, curcumin, and amine-type antioxidants (N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine, N-phenyl-1-naphthylamine, N-phenyl-2-naphthylamine, and diphenylamine) used as propellant preservatives. CQDs were synthesized by reflux method in water-soluble form using molecular precursors, citric acid (CA) and N-(4-methylpyridin-2-yl)ethane-1,2-diamine (NMPE). We could detect picric acid sensitively and selectively with the use these CQDs having a high quantum yield of 46%. Furthermore, this sensor could determine curcumin and amine-type antioxidants with high sensitivity. In the presence of potential interferents (i.e. metal ions, anions, biomolecules, and camouflage materials used to disguise explosives), the method developed for both picric acid and curcumin was not significantly affected. This CQDs−based fluorometric method could be easily applied to the determination of picric acid in sandy soils and for curcumin in propellants.

Citric acid and N-(4-methylpyridin-2-yl)ethane-1,2-diamine used in the synthesis of CQDs were purchased from Sigma. Ethanol (EtOH), methanol (MeOH), acetone, acetonitrile (ACN), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from Merck. Common metal cations (Cd2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, and Ca2+), anions, (Cl−, NO3 −, NO2 −, SO4 2−, and CO3 2−) and biomolecules (glucose, trypsin, lysine, l-phenylalanine, and DL-alanine) used as potential interferents, and stabilizers (curcumin, N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine, N-phenyl-1-naphthylamine, N-phenyl-2-naphthylamine, diphenylamine, gallic acid) were obtained from Sigma. All reagents were of analytical purity unless otherwise stated.

Explosives TNT (pure), 2,4,6-trinitrophenylmethylnitramine (tetryl, pure), picric acid: PA (pure), 1,3,5-trinitroperhydro-1,3,5-triazine (RDX with 85% active compound), 1,3,5,7-tetranitro-1,3,5,7-tetraazacyclooctane (HMX, pure), pentaerythritol tetranitrate (PETN, pure) were provided by the Mechanical Chemical Industry Corporation (Makine Kimya Enstitüsü Kurumu-MKEK) under previous projects. RTC-blended sandy soil (clean sandy soil) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich.

Tris–HCl buffer (at pH 5.5–10.5) solutions were prepared from 1.0 × 10−1 mol L −1 tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane hydrochloride (Tris) and 1.0 × 10−1 mol L −1 HCl. All aqueous solutions used in the experiments were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm, Millipore).

The UV–vis spectra of CQDs, PA, and CUR were obtained with a UV-1900i UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) using 1.0 cm matched quartz cells. The fluorescence spectra of CQDs were collected using a Cary Eclipse spectrometer (Agilent, USA). The infrared spectra were recorded by diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier-transform spectroscopy using an Alpha T model spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany) in the 4000–400 cm–1 range. Raman spectra of CQDs were collected using an InVia microscopic confocal Raman spectrometer (Renishaw, England) under a He–Ne laser with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. Scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) of CQDs were performed using a Quattro FEG ESEM (FEI, USA). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were carried out on a 90Plus Particle Size Analyzer (Brookhaven Instrument, USA) equipped with a 35 mW HeNe laser at a temperature of 25.0 ± 0.2 °C in water. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using a K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) employing a monochromated Al Kα X-ray source (hv = 14,686.6 eV). A Seven Compact S220 pH meter (Mettler Toledo, Switzerland) was utilized to measure the pH values. An LC-20A liquid chromatograph (Shimadzu, Japan) coupled with a LCMS-8040 mass spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used to validate the proposed fluorometric method against a literature LC–MS method. LC was equipped with an Ultra-AQ column (Restek, USA100 × 2.1 mm, 3 μm, C18).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using a simple one-step reflux method [48]. Briefly, citric acid (0.5 g; 2.6 × 10−3 mol), N-(4-methylpyridin-2-yl)ethane-1,2-diamine (0.1 g; 6.6 × 10−4 mol), and ultrapure water (5.0 mL) were added to the 3-neck reaction flask and mixed for homogenization. The flask was then placed in a mantle heater and stirred under reflux for 4 h at 200 °C. The mixture cooled to room temperature was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min, and the supernatant fraction was dialyzed in the dialysis membrane bag for 24 h. Blue emission carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were obtained as a brown solid. The pure CQDs were characterized and used (0.05 g mL−1) in experimental studies.

Picric acid was sensitively determined by spectrofluorometry. Firstly, 0.5 g of CQDs was added to 10.0 mL of ultrapure water to prepare the stock CQDs solution. CQDs solution (400 µL; 0.05 g mL−1), Tris–HCl buffer (100 µL; 1.0 × 10−1 mol L −1; pH 7.0) and increasing amounts of PA solution (10 µL − 200 µL of 2.0 × 10−6 mol L −1 stock picric acid solution) were added to a series of glass vials and final volumes of the prepared solutions were made up to 2.0 mL with MeOHH2O (1:1; v/v) solvent mixture. The solutions were left to stand for 1.0 min for full interaction between CQDs and picric acid, and their fluorescence spectra were recorded in the emission range of 300–600 nm (λex = 310 nm). All experiments were carried out at room temperature. The limit of detection was calculated using the equation LOD = 3σ bl/m (where σ bl was the standard deviation of blank-level measurements, and m the slope of the linear calibration equation).

A stock solution of CQDs was first prepared, similar to the determination of picric acid. Stock CQDs solution (400 µL; 0.05 g mL−1), Tris–HCl buffer (100 µL; 1.0 × 10−1 mol L −1; pH 7.0), and increasing amounts of curcumin (5 µL − 100 µL of 2.0 × 10−4 mol L −1 stock curcumin solution) were added to a series of glass vials and their final volumes were made up to 2.0 mL with MeOHH2O (1:1; v/v) solvent mixture. For the interaction between CQDs and curcumin, the prepared solutions were left to stand for 1 min at room temperature. Fluorescence measurements of the solutions were carried out at room temperature in the emission range of 300–600 nm (λex = 310 nm). LOD was calculated as described for picric acid using the curcumin calibration equation parameters.

A stock solution of CQDs was first prepared, similar to the determination of picric acid. Stock CQDs solution (400 µL; 0.05 g mL−1), Tris–HCl buffer (100 µL; 1.0 × 10−1 mol L −1; pH 7.0), and increasing amounts of amine-type antioxidants (5 µL − 50 µL of 2.0 × 10−4 mol L −1 stock amine-type antioxidant solution, i.e., N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine, N-phenyl-1-naphthylamine, N-phenyl-2-naphthylamine, and diphenylamine) were added to a series of glass vials and their final volumes were made up to 2.0 mL with MeOHH2O (1:1; v/v) solvent mixture. The prepared solutions were left to stand for 1 min to allow interaction between CQDs and amine-type antioxidants. Fluorescence measurements of the solutions were carried out at room temperature in the emission range of 300–600 nm (λex = 310 nm). LOD was calculated as described for picric acid using the amine-stabilizers calibration equation parameters.

For the selective determination of picric acid, a selectivity study was carried out in the presence of different explosives (TNT and tetryl as nitro-aromatics, RDX and HMX as nitramines, and PETN as nitrate-ester). Accordingly, Tris–HCl (100 µL; 1.0 × 10−1 mol L −1; pH 7.0) buffer and different explosives (100 µL; 2.0 × 10−6 mol L −1; PA, TNT, tetryl, RDX, HMX, and PETN) were added separately to glass vials containing stock CQDs solution (400 µL; 0.05 g mL−1). The final volume of all solutions was made up to 2.0 mL with MeOHH2O (1:1; v/v) solvent mixture. The solutions prepared for the interaction between explosives and CQDs were left to stand for 1 min. Fluorescence spectra of all prepared solutions were recorded in the emission range of 300–600 nm (λex = 310 nm).

In addition to the selectivity study for picric acid, the effects of possible interferences (metal cations, anions, explosives, and camouflage materials) that may affect picric acid determination were also investigated. Potential interferents (metal ions and anions at 100-fold; 100 µL; 2.0 × 10−4 mol L −1, explosives and camouflage materials at 10-fold; 100 µL; 2.0 × 10−5 mol L −1) were added to vials containing stock CQDs (400 µL; 0.05 g mL−1) and Tris–HCl (100 µL; 1.0 × 10−1 mol L −1; pH 7.0) buffer, and picric acid (100 µL; 2.0 × 10−6 mol L −1) was added to each vial. Final volumes of the solutions were made up to 2.0 mL with MeOHH2O (1:1; v/v) solvent mixture. Fluorescence spectra of the prepared solutions were recorded in the emission range of 300–600 nm.

To examine the effect of possible interferences (metal ions, anions, and biomolecules) on the determination of propellant stabilizers, curcumin was selected as the best responsive antioxidant. In general, potential interferents (metal cations, anions, and biomolecules at 10-fold; 30 µL; 2.0 × 10−3 mol L −1) were added separately to vials containing stock CQDs (400 µL; 0.05 g mL−1) and Tris–HCl (100 µL; 1.0 × 10−1 mol L −1; pH 7.0) buffer. Finally, curcumin (30 µL; 2.0 × 10−4 mol L −1) was added to all vials and the solutions were made up to 2.0 mL with MeOHH2O (1:1; v/v) solvent mixture. Fluorescence spectra of all prepared solutions were recorded in the emission range of 300–600 nm.

The quantum yield (QY) of CQDs was measured by comparing the fluorescence intensities and absorption values of a solution of CQDs with those of anthracene (excitation wavelength: 365 nm, quantum yield 0.27, dissolved in EtOH) [49]. In an effort to minimize the reabsorption effect, the absorption of the CQDs solution was kept below 0.1. The quantum yield of CQDs was calculated by the following equation (Eq. (1)):
(1)
ϕ S
=
ϕ R
I S
I R
A R
A S
η S 2
η R 2

The symbols “ϕ”, “I”, “η”, and “A” denote quantum yield, measured integrated emission intensity, refractive index of the solvent, and absorbance, respectively. The subscript “S” represents CQDs, and the subscript “R” represents reference standard substance (the refractive index of water was 1.33 and of alcohol was 1.36). The quantum yield of CQDs was found (by Eq. (1)) to be 48.3%. All obtained parameters are summarized in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of picric acid in soil samples
ce:section.ce:para: Since real soil samples as reference standards containing the mentioned explosives are not freely accessible from the chemical suppliers market, soils were artificially contaminated with the mentioned explosives with spikes, and the spiked soils thus prepared were the subjects of analysis with the proposed and reference methods for validation studies. A mass of 2.0 g of sandy soil was weighed and taken into a beaker; this soil was contaminated with a solution of picric acid prepared in acetone (2.5 mL; 4.5 × 10−3 mol L −1), and kept in an ultrasonic bath for 5 min for homogenization. The sandy soil sample was dried at 50 °C to remove the solvent. For PA extraction from sandy soil, the soil sample was washed with 2.0 mL of MeOH, the resulting solution was filtered (CHROMOFIL, PET–20/25), and placed in a 25.0 mL flask. This process was repeated three times for five sandy soil samples and the final volumes of the solutions were made up to 25.0 mL with MeOH. This picric acid extract was diluted 1250-fold, and picric acid was analyzed by the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of curcumin in propellant formulation
ce:section.ce:para: One of the aims of this work was to determine the antioxidant stabilizers in propellant formulations. Accordingly, a typical propellant formulation was prepared by applying the procedure in the literature [50]. To prepare 0.5 g of the propellant composition, nitrocellulose (82%), nitroguanidine (11%), potassium sulfate (1%), dibutylphthalate (3%), graphite (0.4%), calcium carbonate (0.6%), and stabilizer (in this case, curcumin; 2%) were weighed and prepared as a powder mixture in a glass vial. This mixture was dissolved in 10.0 mL of MeOH, 1000-fold diluted, and analyzed by the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method validation against reference LC-MS/MS and CUPRAC methods
ce:section.ce:para: The liquid chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS/MS) method [51] and the CUPRAC method [52] were used for validation of the developed fluorometric nanoprobe for picric acid and curcumin detection, respectively. All experimental details of the reference LC–MS/MS and CUPRAC assays are given in the “Supplementary Material”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and optical properties of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were prepared by one-step reflux treatment [48] for 4 h at 200 °C under optimized conditions (using citric acid as carbon source and N-(4-methylpyridin-2-yl)ethane-1,2-diamine as nitrogen source) as shown in Scheme 1 . The first solution, which was light yellow, turned brown during the refluxing process. At the end of this period, the solution cooled to room temperature was dialyzed in a cellulose membrane bag (MWCO 3500) for 24 h. Pure CQDs were obtained by drying the brown solid formed in the membrane in a vacuum oven at 40 °C.

The optical properties of CQDs were investigated by fluorescence and UV–vis spectroscopy. Fig. 1 a shows the excitation, emission, and absorption spectra of CQDs and the absorption spectra of picric acid and curcumin as the target analytes. CQDs exhibit strong blue fluorescence at a wavelength of 380 nm (λex = 310 nm). However, it is clearly seen in daylight that the CQDs solution is almost colorless and glows bright blue under UV-lamp (λex = 365 nm) (Fig. 1a-inset). In the absorption spectrum of CQDs, peaks centered at 210 nm and 305 nm wavelengths were obtained. The structure of CQDs consists of both the graphite layer and heteroatoms functionalized around the graphite layer. The absorption peak at 210 nm may be ascribed to the n-π* transition of the citric acid carboxylic acid group [53], whereas the absorption peak at 305 nm may be due to the n-π* electronic transition of the bonds between the heteroatoms functionalized on the graphitic surface and the carbon atom (i.e., C = O and C = N) [54]. In addition, the absorption and excitation bands of CQDs show almost an exact overlap. Except for CQDs, when the picric acid, curcumin, and N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine absorption bands of the three analytes are examined, the maximum absorption peaks of picric acid emerge at 305 nm, curcumin 320 nm, and N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine at 400 nm wavelengths. The absorption bands of picric acid and curcumin analytes partially overlap with the absorption and excitation bands of CQDs. The absorption spectrum of N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine mostly overlaps with the absorption and excitation band of CQDs.

In order to determine the photostability of the CQDs solution used in the experimental studies, the emission intensity of the solution at 380 nm wavelength was recorded day by day. Fluorescence intensity measurements of the prepared solution was performed, and the solution was stored in the refrigerator at 5 °C. CQDs solution showed excellent photostability up to 10 days with relative intensities ranging from 99% to 101%; after the 10th day, measurement was taken on the 15th day to observe a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 1b).

CQDs synthesized and purified under optimized conditions were characterized by STEM, DLS, IR, Raman, and XPS techniques. For the morphological structural investigation of CQDs, they were dropped on a copper grid and STEM images were obtained (Fig. 2 a) to show that CQDs have a spherical structure and different sizes. The spherical structure of CQDs can be seen more clearly in Fig. 2a inset. As a result of these different sized structures, particle size analysis was performed in CQDs solution. In the DLS histogram given in Fig. 2b, it was understood that the particle sizes of CQDs were between 0.5 nm and 3.5 nm in a well-dispersed solution medium, with a maximum frequency of up to 2.0 nm particles.

The infrared spectrum of CQDs is shown in Fig. 2c. NH and OH stretching vibrations at 3624 cm−1 and 3312 cm−1, respectively, are characteristic absorption bands. The 2340 cm−1 peak can be attributed to the CO2 gas adsorbed from air [55]. The C = O stretching vibration of the carboxylate group functionalized on the CQDs surface is observed at 1715 cm−1 and the NH bending vibration of the amine group functionalized on the surface is seen at 1547 cm−1. The peak at 1594 cm−1 confirms the C = C bond in the graphite layer. CN and CO bending vibrations indicate the hetero-atom functionalization of graphite, which are manifested in the peaks of 1223 cm−1 and 1143 cm−1, respectively [56]. In addition, these peaks are an indication of the favorable properties CQDs such as water stability and dispersibility. Finally, the CH bending vibration emerging at 901 cm−1 is confirmed. These findings reveal that the graphitic layer of synthesized CQDs also contains amine and carboxylic groups on the surface.

Fig. 2d shows the Raman spectrum of CQDs to display the G-band at 1563 cm−1 and d-band at 1341 cm−1. Of these Raman shift bands, the G-band can be attributed to sp3-hybridization and the d-band to sp2-hybridization [57]. In addition, the ratio of d- to G-band intensities (ID/IG) of defective carbon atoms can be used as a measure of the degree of graphitization. The ID/IG ratio of our carbon dots is 0.59, which supports the high amount of surface defects on the CQDs surface [58].

In addition, the analysis of the elements functionalized on the surface of CQDs, and their chemical bonds are explained by XPS. As shown in the survey spectrum given in Fig. 3 a, there are three major elements on the surface of CQDs, namely C, N, and O. The contents of C, N, and O elements were determined as 66.84%, 19.30%, and 13.86%, respectively. Four peaks are seen in the high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s in Fig. 3b. The peak at 284.98 eV indicates the C = C bond of CQDs in the graphite structure. The peak at 287.48 eV belongs to the CN bond, confirming the functionalization of the amine (CNH2) group at the surface. There is a peak of C = N bond at 288.78 eV, attributable to the pyridine ring in the graphite structure. The peak observed at 291.68 eV confirms the CO bond in the carboxylate (-COOH) and hydroxyl (-OH) groups functionalized on the surface. There are 3 peaks in the O1s spectrum in Fig. 3c. Peaks of 533.38 eV, 534.68 eV and 536.08 eV belong to OH, O = C, and OC bonds, respectively. These peaks confirm the functionalized hydroxyl (-OH) and carboxylate (-COOH) groups on the surface. Finally, when the N1s spectrum is examined, 3 different peaks are seen (Fig. 3d). The peaks of NH, N = C/NC, and NO bonds are observed at 399.38 eV, 401.78 eV, and 406.78 eV, respectively. The NH peak confirms the functionalized amine (CNH2) group on the surface, and the N = C/NC peak confirms the pyridine ring in the graphite layer. The 406.78 eV peak is attributable to oxidized nitrogen and possibly belongs to the NO bond of the nitrate group [59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of working conditions of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were synthesized by one-step reflux method. The carbon and nitrogen sources used in the synthesis were subjected to pyrolysis. After the pyrolysis process, the formation of a graphitic carbon layer and the functionalization of heteroatoms on the surface occurred. Thus, the reaction temperature and time required optimization for the best CQDs synthesis. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs at 380 nm emission wavelength first increased (from 100 °C to 200 °C) and then decreased (from 200 °C to 300 °C) with increasing temperature. This can be attributed to the degradation of the surface structure of CQDs at very high temperatures [60]. The optimum reaction temperature was determined as 200 °C, and CQDs synthesis was carried out for different reaction times at this temperature. When the reaction time was increased from 2 h to 4 h, the fluorescence intensity increased first, and then decreased at longer reaction times (Fig. S2). Thus, the optimum reaction time was determined as 4 h.

The optimal excitation wavelength was investigated for CQDs synthesized under optimum conditions. Emission spectra of the CQDs solution were recorded at 10 nm intervals from 260 nm to 340 nm of excitation wavelengths. As seen in Fig. S3, the highest fluorescence intensity was recorded at 310 nm excitation wavelength. In addition, the fluorescence properties of solutions of CQDs prepared in different solvents were investigated. As shown in Fig. S4, when the fluorescence intensities of CQDs solutions prepared in ultrapure water, different organic solvents, and their admixtures mixtures with H2O were examined, the highest fluorescence intensity was observed in ultrapure water. The closest fluorescence intensity to that in ultrapure water was observed in the MeOHH2O (1:1, v/v) mixture. Thus, the working solution was chosen as MeOHH2O (1:1, v/v) mixture, as methanol was necessary to dissolve the antioxidants selectively targeted by the CQDs probe.

Finally, pH was tested by using different Tris–HCl buffers prepared in the pH range of 5.5 − 10.5, and trials were performed for CQDs alone, the (CQDs+picric acid) system, and the (CQDs+curcumin) system. When the fluorescence intensity versus pH curves (Fig. S5) were examined, it was seen that CQDs maintained a high intensity over a wide pH interval, while the (CQDs+picric acid) and (CQDs+curcumin) binary systems displayed minimum intensities between pH 7.0 and 8.0, showing that the intensity difference achieved by fluorescence quenching was maximal within this interval. Since amine-type antioxidants were also determined within the scope of this study, the working pH was selected as 7.5.

To determine the sensitivity of CQDs in our detection system, a series of CQDs solutions were prepared with increasing concentrations of picric acid (1.0 × 10−8 mol L −1 to 2.0 × 10−7 mol L −1). Then, the fluorescence spectra of the prepared solutions were recorded. As seen in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs decreased smoothly with increasing picric acid concentrations. The fluorescence intensities of CQDs at the wavelength of 380 nm (i.e., emission maximum) were used to prepare the linear calibration curve, showing excellent linearity in the concentration range of 1.0 × 10−8 mol L −1 to 1.4 × 10−7 mol L −1 of picric acid (Fig. 4b). The equation for the linear variation of CQDs intensity quenching (∆I) with picric acid micromolar concentration (C PA in μmol L −1) is given as:
∆I = I 0–I = (3740 ± 2) C PA + (50.4 ± 3 × 10−2) (N = 14, r = 0.996)
where I 0 and I represent the fluorescence intensities at 380 nm in the presence and absence of picric acid, respectively. The detection limit for PA was found to be 0.5 nmol L −1, and the coefficients of variation (C.V.) were 0.9% (intra–assay) and 1.0% (inter–assay).

In addition, the binding constant (Ka) of the interaction of CQDs with the target analyte picric acid was determined by applying the Benesi-Hildebrand (B-H) method [61]. The Benesi-Hildebrand plot was created with the B-H equation given below (Eq. (2)).
(2)
1
(
I 0
− I )
=
1
(
I 0
−
I ′
)

P A
]

(

I ′
)
where Ka represents the binding constant, I 0 the fluorescence intensity of CQDs, I’ the intensity resulting from the interaction of CQDs with picric acid, and I the intensity observed at the maximum. The coupling constant was precisely calculated as 1.2 × 103 L mol−1 from the slope and intercept values of the double-reciprocal B-H plot [62] given in Fig. 4c. The calculated Ka value indicates the stability of the interaction between CQDs and picric acid in the ground state, i.e., without excitation. This relative stability presumably results from the electrostatic attraction between the cationic ammonium groups on CQDs and picrate anion (pKa of picric acid is 0.4, i.e., picric acid is distinctly anionic at the working pH), added to the hydrogen-bonding interaction involving a H-bonded proton-transfer complex N +–H…O − [63] between CQDs and picric acid.

In addition, the selectivity of CQDs for detecting the target analyte: picric acid was investigated. Various explosive compounds (tetryl, TNT, PETN, HMX, and RDX) were used to evaluate CQDs selectivity toward picric acid. As seen in Fig. 5 a (fluorescence spectra) and Fig. 5b (intensities histogram), CQDs show high sensitivity for picric acid only, possibly because these nitro-explosives are devoid of easily ionizable phenolic –OH groups that may form H-bonded complexes with CQDs. These CQDs also respond at a minor level to TNT. This can be explained by the fact that TNT forms weak Meisenheimer anion [64] complexes at the working pH of 7.5 (i.e., normal complex formation is expected at elevated pH in the alkaline region), and this anion inappreciably interacts with CQDs.

Curcumin detection was investigated using CQDs under optimal experimental conditions. When the emission spectra given in Fig. 6 a are examined, it is seen that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs gradually decreases with increasing curcumin concentrations. As a result of this smooth decrease, fluorescence intensities recorded at increasing curcumin concentrations (0.5 × 10−6 mol L −1 to 5.0 × 10−6 mol L −1) at 380 nm wavelength were plotted to determine the linear relationship between CQDs and curcumin (Fig. 6b). As the three pKa values of curcumin in the ground state were reported to be 8.4, 9.9 and 10.5, curcumin is basically not anionic at the working pH, and the good selectivity of CQDs toward curcumin may be interpreted by H-bonding interactions between them (i.e., actually curcumin is only 11.2% monoanionic at the working pH of 7.5 giving rise to a weak electrostatic attraction). Although the cis-enol form is the most stable conformer of curcumin in the ground state due to intramolecular H-bonding stabilization, the possibility of intermolecular H-bonding at the site of phenolic –OH groups cannot be ruled out [65].

An excellent linear relationship was observed between curcumin and CQDs in the 0.5 × 10−6 mol L −1 to 5.0 × 10−6 mol L −1 concentration range. The equation of the formed linear line is as given as:
∆I = I 0–I = (31.5 ± 2 × 10−2) C CUR + (1.6 ± 5 × 10−1) (N = 10, r = 0.998)
where I 0 and I represent fluorescence intensities at 380 nm in the presence and absence of curcumin, respectively, and C represents the final micromolar concentration (μmol L −1) of curcumin. The detection limit for curcumin was found to be 70 nmol L −1, and the coefficients of variation (C.V.) were 0.7% (intra–assay) and 1.2% (inter–assay).

The binding constant (Ka) of the interaction of CQDs with the selective analyte curcumin was calculated by the Benesi-Hildebrand (B-H) equation. The relevant Benesi-Hildebrand plot was created using the equation given in Eq (2). The binding constant was calculated as 3.3 × 102 L mol−1 using the slope and intercept values of the linear B-H graph given in Fig. 6c. The calculated Ka value indicates the stability of the interaction between CQDs and curcumin without excitation, as in the case of picric acid; however, this is a weaker interaction (than that of CQDs with PA) due to the absence of electrostatic factor in the association.

N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine, N-phenyl-1-naphthylamine, N-phenyl-2-naphthylamine, and diphenylamine were used as amine-type antioxidants. For N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine detection sensitivity, increasing concentrations of N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine (0.5 × 10−6 mol L −1 to 5.0 × 10−5 mol L −1) solution were added to a series of vials containing CQDs solution, and the fluorescence spectra of all prepared solutions were recorded. It may be seen that the fluorescence of CQDs decreases with increasing N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine concentration (Fig. 7 a). The relationship of the intensity decrease with N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine concentration is given in Fig. 7b, revealing a linear correlation in the range of 1.0 × 10−6 mol L −1 to 4.5 × 10−6 mol L −1 concentrations. The line equation for this relationship is:
∆I = I 0–I = (30.3 ± 3.8 × 10−2) C PPDa + (21.8 ± 4.1 × 10−2) (N = 8, r = 0.998)
where I 0 and I represent fluorescence intensities at 380 nm in the presence and absence of N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine, respectively, and C represents the final micromolar concentration (μmol L −1) of N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine. The detection limit for N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine was found to be 600 nmol L −1, and the coefficients of variation (C.V.) were 0.9% (intra–assay) and 1.0% (inter–assay).

As for other analytes, the binding constant Ka was calculated to be 2.9 × 102 L mol−1 for N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine using the double-reciprocal B-H plot given in Fig. 7c. Comparison of the binding constants for relevant analytes yields an order of picric acid>curcumin>N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine, also indicating their fluorescence quenching ability. This is related to the extent of electrostatic interactions and H-bonding (i.e., only the latter interaction is valid for curcumin and amine stabilizers).

In addition, similar results were obtained in the presence of other amine-type antioxidants (N-phenyl-1-naphthylamine, N-phenyl-2-naphthylamine, and diphenylamine). Spectra, tables, and the necessary explanations in the presence of these antioxidants are given in the “Detection of amine-type antioxidants” section of the "Supplementary Material" (Figs. S6-S8, and Table S2). The validation parameters for the analytical performance of the developed sensor for picric acid, curcumin, and N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine determination are summarized in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism study of CQDs with picric acid, curcumin, and N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine system
ce:section.ce:para: When the absorption spectrum of the analyte (fluorescence quencher) overlaps with the fluorescence excitation or emission spectra of the sensor (fluorescent substance or fluorophore), the analyte absorbs the excitation or emission radiation of the sensor with the inner filter effect (IFE), and the fluorescence of the sensor is effectively quenched [66]. To understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism between CQDs and analytes (picric acid, curcumin, and N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine), it is necessary to examine in detail the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra and absorption spectra given in Fig. 1a. It is seen that the absorption spectra of picric acid and curcumin highly overlap with the excitation spectra of CQDs; in addition, the absorption spectra of picric acid and curcumin overlap with the emission spectrum of CQDs. On the other hand, the absorption spectrum of N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine has a high overlap with the emission spectrum of CQDs and a partial overlap with the excitation spectrum of CQDs. These observations suggest that the fluorescence quenching of CQDs by the target analytes results from the inner filter effect mechanism.

Static quenching usually results from the formation of complex molecules between the fluorophore and the quencher analyte devoid of ground state fluorescence [67], whereas dynamic quenching, on the contrary, is associated with the collision between the fluorophore and the quencher in the excited state (i.e., no complex formation is observed in the ground state). Inner filter effect is associated with static quenching. To confirm this situation, time dependent fluorescence spectra of CQDs, (CQDs+picric acid), (CQDs+curcumin) and (CQDs+N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) systems were recorded; their fluorescence lifetime spectra are given in Fig. 8 a, 8b and 8c, and average fluorescence lifetimes (τa) were calculated as 7.32±0.04 ns, 7.34±0.03 ns, 7.33±0.05 ns, and 7.39±0.03 ns, respectively. The closeness of these values indicates that the fluorescence quenching mechanism between CQDs and target analytes (picric acid, curcumin, and N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) is static. In accordance with this, another evidence is that the Stern-Volmer graphs given in Fig. 8a-inset, 8b-inset, and 8c-inset lack linearity, confirming the nature of the interaction between CQDs and analytes is static quenching.

These findings revealed that the fluorescence quenching of CQDs in the presence of target analytes (picric acid, curcumin, and N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) arises from the inner filter effect, involving static quenching, and the schematic representation for picric acid, curcumin, and N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine sensing with CQDs is shown in Scheme 2 .

The effects of possible interferents on the selective determination of picric acid and curcumin were investigated for selected metal cations, anions and camouflage materials as described in “2.7. Selectivity and Interference Study”. picric acid was added to each vial by adding metal ions and anions 100-fold (of picric acid concentration) and camouflage materials 10-fold (of picric acid concentration) separately to the vials containing CQDs solution. The fluorescence intensities of each solution at the emission wavelength of 380 nm were recorded. Examining the bar graph in Fig. 9 a reveals that CQDs without picric acid (orange bars) and with picric acid (green bars) performed excellently and potential interferents did not show an adverse effect in analysis. In the presence of these interferents, recovery in fluorescence of CQDs without picric acid was between 96% and 104%, and recovery in fluorescence of CQDs with picric acid was between 95% and 104% (Table S3). However, explosive traces can be found together in a post-blast debris or in remediated land subject to explosive contamination. Explosives other than picric acid cannot show the ability to quench the fluorescence of CQDs due to their incompetence of ground-state complex formation with CQDs. Considering the values given in Table 2 , it is seen that other explosives (tetryl, TNT, PETN, HMX, and RDX) that may coexist with picric acid did not have a significant effect on the probe selectivity toward picric acid up to 10-fold, with recovery values between 95% and 104%, meaning that this method can well distinguish picric acid from other nitro-explosives without confusion.

Similarly, metal ions (Cd2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, and Ca2+), anions Cl−, NO3 −, NO2 −, SO4 2−, and CO3 2−), and biomolecules (glucose, trypsin, lysine, l-phenylalanine, and DL-alanine) were tested as potential interferents for curcumin. Metal cations, anions, and biomolecules were added 10-fold (based on curcumin concentration) separately to vials containing CQDs solution. Finally, the fluorescence intensities at 380 nm emission wavelength of the solutions prepared by adding curcumin to each vial were examined. The histogram plot in Fig. 9b shows that the tested substances do not have a significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs without curcumin (blue bar) and CQDs with curcumin (pink bar). Good recovery between 97% and 104% was obtained in the fluorescence intensity of CQDs in the presence of interferents without curcumin, and between 95% and 105% in the presence of interferents with curcumin (Table S4). In addition, antioxidants are found in food products in mixtures. For this reason, to investigate the effect of other antioxidants on curcumin determination, double (curcumin+N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine and curcumin+GA) and triple (curcumin+N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine+diphenylamine and curcumin+gallic acid+diphenylamine) mixtures were prepared by adding different antioxidants in addition to curcumin. Examining Table 3 , a good recovery between 95% and 104% was obtained for the determination of curcumin in the presence of antioxidant mixtures. This shows that the proposed method enables accurate curcumin analysis in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CQDs for real samples
Detection of PA in soil sample
ce:section.ce:para: As appropriate certified reference materials (CRMs) are not always available, other materials having a sufficiently complex matrix may be used to provide a control on overall method bias; a further alternative is to use spiking and recovery information [68]. The interferent response to the developed analytical method (relative to the analyte response) can be estimated by application of the procedure on a matrix blank and the same blank spiked with the potential interferent at a suitable concentration; soil is a sufficiently complex sample for spiking [69].

For determining picric acid in soil extract, 2.0 g of sandy soil was contaminated with 2.5 mL acetone solution of picric acid, and the solvent was removed from the soil by letting to dry up under daylight. Then, picric acid was re-extracted with acetonitrile and the working solution was prepared by appropriate dilutions. This procedure was applied to 5 different soil samples. The obtained picric acid solutions were analyzed with the proposed method to yield recoveries between 97 and 104%. The average recovery from 5 different soil samples was 102%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CUR in propellant composition
ce:section.ce:para: The normal curcumin content of solutions prepared from standard propellant formulations could be found by the proposed method after appropriate dilution (in this case, 1000-fold dilution was made). This procedure was repeated for 5 different propellant compositions. The performed analyses yielded curcumin recoveries ranging from 102% to 104% (average recovery was 103% from 5 propellant samples).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of developed method against LC-MS/MS and CUPRAC methods
ce:section.ce:para:
PA extracted from 5 sandy soil samples contaminated with identical amounts of PA was analyzed by the reference LC-MS/MS method in the literature [51]. Before the real sample analysis, the prepared working solutions (0.4 × 10−6, 0.8 × 10−6, 1.7 × 10−6, 3.5 × 10−6, 4.4 × 10−6 mol L −1) were analyzed with LC-MS/MS by constructing a calibration line as below:
Peak Area = 3.1 × 106 C PA + 6.2 × 102 (N = 5, r = 0.997)

Then, the PA solutions extracted from sandy soil samples were analyzed by LC-MS/MS and their concentrations calculated using the above equation. Statistical comparison of picric acid values found with both the proposed and reference methods are depicted in Table 4 .

Curcumin is an antioxidant compound well responding to the CUPRAC antioxidant assay. Curcumin in 5 different propellant compositions was determined with the CUPRAC method; while the curcumin phenolic groups were oxidized to the corresponding quinones, the CUPRAC reagent was reduced to the yellow-colored cuprous-neocuproine chelate responsible for absorption at 450 nm [48]. First, increasing amounts of curcumin (0.5 × 10−6, 1.0 × 10−6, 1.5 × 10−6, 2.0 × 10−6, and 2.5 × 10−6 mol L −1) were added to the CUPRAC reagent in order to prepare the calibration line, the final mixtures were kept at room temperature for 30 min for color development, and their visible spectra taken. The calibration line for curcumin was:
A 450nm = 6.9 × 10−1 C CUR - 2.7 × 10−3 (N = 5, r = 0.999)

This method was applied to 5 different propellant samples (of the same composition) dissolved and diluted as appropriate, and the unknown curcumin content of formulation was calculated using the above equation. Statistical comparison of curcumin contents found with both the proposed and reference methods are depicted in Table 4.

In this study, CQDs with high quantum yield (48.3%) were synthesized by the simple reflux method using citric acid and N-(4-methylpyridin-2-yl)ethane-1,2-diamine as raw materials. The obtained CQDs with excellent water solubility were characterized by various instrumental techniques such as STEM, IR, XPS; these CQDs had a spherical structure of 2.0 nm average diameter, having surface doped with a high amount of nitrogen and oxygen. The polymeric graphite structure of CQDs was determined by Raman spectroscopy. Under optimized conditions, CQDs can detect picric acid with high sensitivity and selectively. The limit of detection (LOD) for picric acid was calculated to be 0.5 nmol L −1 which was lower than that most fluorometric methods. In addition to picric acid, the determination of antioxidants (curcumin and amine-type antioxidants) used as stabilizer in propellant formulations was carried out. Curcumin and amine-type antioxidants (N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine, N-phenyl-1-naphthylamine, N-phenyl-2-naphthylamine, diphenylamine) were determined with high precision. The LOD for curcumin was 70 nmol L −1 and for amine type antioxidants, LOD values were: 600 nmol L −1, for N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine, 800 nmol L −1, for N-phenyl-1-naphthylamine, 840 nmol L −1, for N-phenyl-2-naphthylamine, 920 nmol L −1, for diphenylamine. The fluorescence quenching phenomenon of CQDs in the presence of target analytes (picric acid, curcumin, and N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) was explained by the inner filter effect mechanism arising from complex formation in the ground state, using fluorescence lifetime analyses and Stern-Volmer plots. These complexes resulted from basically electrostatic interactions of CQDs with picric acid, and H-bonding interactions with other analytes. The effect of metal cations, anions, biomolecules, and camouflage materials, which may interfere with the determination of picric acid and curcumin, was investigated and the analytes in the presence of these interferences were shown to be recoverable at ratios ranging between 95% and 105%. Moreover, synthetic mixtures of explosives for picric acid and of antioxidants for curcumin were prepared, and picric acid and curcumin were accurately determined in these mixtures not to be adversely affected at 95% confidence level. Finally, different sandy soil samples were contaminated with picric acid, and the picric acid extracted from these samples was analyzed by both the CQDs-based fluorescence method and reference LC-MS/MS method. In addition, a propellant composition containing curcumin as stabilizer was prepared as described in literature, and curcumin was analyzed using both the proposed and reference CUPRAC antioxidant measurement method. Successful usability of the manufactured CQDs for picric acid and curcumin determinations, validated against reference methods for real sample analyses, was confirmed by the results obtained. This study showed that CQDs constitute a potential nano-fluorophore for detecting picric acid in soil samples and curcumin (and other amine-type stabilizers) in propellant formulations. Traces of picric acid as a widely used explosive since World War-I can be potentially analyzed in the field with this portable probe in conjunction with a portable fluorometer. On the other hand, there are very few methods in literature for selectively analyzing antioxidant stabilizers used to preserve ammunition, and the proposed method may be important as a rare solution of determining these stabilizers in explosive and propellant formulations.

Elif Ezgi Benli: Experimental work. Ömer Kaan Koç: Experimental work, investigation of optimal parameters, writing. Ayşem Üzer: Method optimization, writing- original draft preparation. Reşat Apak: Conceptualization, writing-reviewing and editing.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Elif Ezgi Benli: She received her bachelor's degree from Istanbul University-Cerrahpasa, Faculty of Engineering, Department of Chemistry in 2022. Her research interests have recently focused on the development of nanomaterial-based sensors for the determination of environmentally and biologically important compounds, and energetic materials.

PhD student Ömer Kaan Koç: He received her master's degree from Çanakkale Onsekiz Mart University, Faculty of Science and Letters, Department of Chemistry in 2017. He is currently continuing her doctoral studies in the field of "Smart and Innovative Materials" within the scope of YÖK 100/2000 Doctoral Scholarship Program at Istanbul University-Cerrahpaşa Graduate Education Institute. His research interests have recently focused on the development of nanomaterial-based sensors for the determination of environmentally and biologically important compounds, and energetic materials.

Prof. Dr. Ayşem Üzer: In 1999 she graduated from Istanbul University Faculty of Engineering, received her doctoral degree in 2009. She is still working as a professor at Istanbul University-Cerrahpaşa Engineering Faculty, Chemistry Department, Analytical Chemistry Division. She has been appointed to the Department of Analytical Chemistry as the Head of the Department as of February 2021. Since the same date, she has been the Deputy Manager of the İÜC Food Antioxidants Measurement Application and Research Center. Her academic studies and expertise are mainly on the field analysis of energetic materials, other environmentally harmful substances and antioxidants by spectroscopic and electroanalytical methods and the development of nanosensors for the determination of these substances.

Prof. Dr. Reşat Apak: Dr. Reşat Apak obtained Ph.D. (1982), became full professor (1993) of analytical chemistry in İstanbul University. He is the editorial board member of Talanta, J. Anal. Chem., Turkish J. Chem. and Anal. Lett. He represents Turkey in IUPAC (Division V). He has authored 300 peer-reviewed articles, 17 book chapters, 4 textbooks, receiving 17704 citations in Google Scholar. He is the inventor of the “CUPRAC (cupric reducing antioxidant capacity) assay”, and the recipient of TUBITAK (Turkish Scientific & Technical Research Council) Science Award (2017) as the highest science award in Turkey. He was elected as a Fellow Member of the Royal Society of Chemistry (2018).
